metadata.seed: I feel calm most of the time, but sometimes when small things pile up it feels like nothing is going the way it should, and I end up blowing up.

patient.query: I feel calm most of the time, but sometimes when small things pile up it feels like nothing is going the way it should, and I end up blowing up.

automatic_thoughts: nothing is going the way it should

llm_response.response: It sounds like the accumulation of small tasks can make you feel like everything is off track, which can be overwhelming. What evidence do you have that things are truly not going as they should, and are there moments when they are working out, even if they seem minor? How might you view those moments differently when the pressure builds?

patient.query: Yeah, it just feels like I'm always chasing after things, you know? Like, I can’t keep up. I guess there are little things that go okay, but they seem so insignificant compared to everything else. When I'm feeling like this, I just focus on what’s not working. It’s hard to see the good moments when I’m under so much pressure.

automatic_thoughts: always chasing after things
can’t keep up
little things seem insignificant
focus on what’s not working
hard to see the good moments

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck in a cycle where you think you’re always falling behind and that nothing matters. What evidence do you have that you can’t keep up, and can you think of any moments—small or big—when you actually did meet a goal or felt a sense of progress? How might looking at those moments change the way you view the pressure you’re under?

patient.query: I mean, I guess there have been times when I’ve accomplished things, like finishing a project at work or even just getting through a tough week. But those moments feel so fleeting, like they don’t balance out the frustration I usually feel. It’s like I have this weight on me, and even when I see something good, it just doesn’t stick. I don’t know if looking at

automatic_thoughts: those moments feel fleeting
they don’t balance out the frustration
I have this weight on me
even when I see something good, it just doesn’t stick

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight even when you finish a project or get through a tough week. What evidence do you have that these successes don’t help at all? Could there be moments, however small, where they do feel meaningful, and how might you notice those instead?

patient.query: I guess I just often think about the next thing I have to tackle, you know? Like as soon as I finish something, I’m already anxious about what’s coming next. It’s hard to appreciate those little wins when it feels like I’m always just… behind. I mean, sure, there are moments that feel nice when I do something well, but they fade so quickly. I just

automatic_thoughts: I'm always just behind
I can't appreciate little wins

llm_response.response: Feeling like you’re always behind can make it hard to savor the wins you do have. What evidence do you have that you’re actually behind, and can you think of times when you’ve felt on track or even ahead? Exploring those moments might help you see a more balanced view of your progress.

patient.query: Yeah, I see what you mean. It’s just hard for me to remember those moments. I know I’ve had times when things felt manageable or even good, but they feel so distant, like my brain just skips over them. Usually, I get so caught up in what’s not done and what I could’ve done better. I guess I keep comparing myself to this ideal of what I should

automatic_thoughts: things felt manageable or even good feel distant
I get so caught up in what’s not done
I could’ve done better
I keep comparing myself to this ideal of what I should

neutral: Amnesia for day to day facts (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where the past feels out of reach and you’re focused on what’s missing. What evidence do you have that your brain is actually “skipping” those moments, versus maybe just not recalling them right now? How might you view those memories if you treated them as simply part of a larger, more balanced picture of your experiences?

patient.query: I don’t know, maybe it's just easier to remember the struggles than the good stuff. I mean, when I think back, it’s almost like those happy memories get overshadowed by everything else. It’s like my mind just jumps to the failures or the things I wish I did differently. I guess if I tried to look at it as just part of a bigger picture… maybe I could find

automatic_thoughts: it's easier to remember the struggles than the good stuff
happy memories get overshadowed by everything else
my mind jumps to the failures
things I wish I did differently

llm_response.response: Do you notice any moments when a positive memory surfaces on its own? What details stand out when that happens, and how does it feel compared to the negative recollections? Exploring those instances might help you see a more balanced view of your past.

patient.query: Um, there are moments that pop up sometimes, like when I’m with friends or just enjoying a quiet moment. I can almost hear their laughter or feel that sense of ease. But then, usually, that warmth gets clouded by this nagging feeling that it won't last or that I have to jump back to reality. Compared to the negative memories, it feels lighter, but it’s almost like

automatic_thoughts: it won't last
I have to jump back to reality

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re concerned that the warmth you feel will fade quickly. What evidence supports that it will end, and what evidence might suggest it could persist or still be meaningful even if it changes?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it’s just this pattern I’ve gotten used to. Like, when I'm feeling good, I start worrying about what might go wrong or when the next stressor will hit. But then again, I can remember moments where I felt happy and they did stick with me in a way, like they popped back up when I needed them. It’s just hard to hold onto that hope

triggers: worrying about what might go wrong
automatic_thoughts: I start worrying about what might go wrong
it's hard to hold onto that hope

contradiction: Tends to be stable in affect (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve noticed that hope can slip away, yet it also resurfaces when you need it. What moments have shown hope staying, and what moments have shown it fading? Exploring those examples might help you see whether the belief that hope can’t be held is truly accurate.

patient.query: I mean, I guess there are times when I’ve really felt connected, like when I was celebrating a friend’s success or just hanging out and laughing. Those moments felt genuine, but then afterward, I’d go back to feeling overwhelmed by everything waiting for me. It’s frustrating because I want to hold onto that feeling of connection and happiness, but it just feels so temporary. I don’t know

automatic_thoughts: I want to hold onto that feeling of connection and happiness
it just feels so temporary

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re frustrated when the good moments seem to slip away quickly. What evidence do you have that the connection and happiness might last longer than it feels? Could there be another way to view those moments that might help you hold onto them a bit more?

patient.query: I guess I can see how some of those good moments pop up in my mind later on, even when I’m feeling down. Like, sometimes I can remember a joke that made me laugh or a time we all just hung out together and felt really comfortable. But it’s like I can't help but push them aside when the stress hits. It’s hard to think that those moments could really stick when

automatic_thoughts: I can't help but push them aside
those moments could really stick

llm_response.response: What makes you feel you have to push those memories away when stress hits? How do you know that letting them stay would change how you feel? What might happen if you allowed a positive memory to stay for a few minutes during a stressful moment?
